Proposed SO-EBM is a new end-to-end stochastic optimization method using an energy-based model.
SO-EBM models the algorithmic predict-and-optimize structure similar to DFL but with some differences.
A differentiable optimization layer is stacked on top of a neural predictor.
SO-EBM eliminates the need for using KKT conditions to induce an implicit differentiable optimization layer.
An explicit energy-based differentiable layer is parameterized as a surrogate to the original optimization problem by leveraging expressive EBMs.
SO-EBM directly models the conditional probability of the decisions using an explicit energy-based differentiable layer as a surrogate.
SO-EBM is designed with two complementary learning objectives to better approximate the optimization landscape with the EBM surrogate.
SO-EBM is not constrained to convex objectives and can be applied to a wide class of stochastic optimization problems.

SO-EBM's computational efficiency comes from the parameterization by an energy-based model, which eliminates the need of solving and differentiating through the optimization procedure at each training iteration.
SO-EBM estimates the derivatives of the energy-based surrogate model using a self-normalized importance sampler.
A Gaussian-mixture proposal distribution is designed for the self-normalized importance sampler.
The importance sampler design involves a Gaussian-mixture proposal distribution to reduce sampling variance and speed up training.
The idea of contrastive divergence for MCMC methods is borrowed to speed up the training of the importance sampler.
A new end-to-end stochastic optimization method based on energy-based model is proposed, avoiding the need to solve and differentiate through optimization problems during training.
A coupled training objective is proposed to encourage the energy-based surrogate to approximate the optimization landscape well.
The energy-based surrogate aims to approximate the optimization landscape effectively.
An efficient self-normalized importance sampler is based on a mixture-of-Gaussian proposal for effective sampling.

A common practice is to compare two-stage learning with Decision Focused Learning (DFL).

Two-stage learning and Decision Focused Learning (DFL) are two different approaches to solving stochastic optimization problems. Two-stage learning involves a predict-then-optimize framework, while DFL integrates prediction and optimization into an end-to-end model.

edge: Two-stage learning may suffer from suboptimal performance due to the misalignment of the prediction loss and the optimization loss, which can affect the overall efficiency of the process.

Decision Focused Learning (DFL) integrates prediction and optimization into an end-to-end model, tailoring the predictive model for the optimization task, thus optimizing the overall efficiency of the process.

OptNet addresses the challenge of computing the Jacobian needed for gradient descent methods. It assumes quadratic optimization objectives and differentiates through the KKT conditions using the implicit function theorem to obtain the Jacobian.
OptNet can obtain the Jacobian by solving the optimization problem along with a set of linear equations in each training iteration.
OptNet extends to more general cases of convex optimization using disciplined parametrized programming (DPP) grammar.

The effectiveness of using the energy-based surrogate function to approximate the optimization landscape is verified

The effectiveness of using the energy-based surrogate function to approximate the optimization landscape is verified. Dropping either the MLE or KLD term leads to a performance degradation in the method.
Without the MLE term, the task loss increases by 13%.

Without the KLD term, the task loss increases by 2%. It is observed that accurately recovering the entire optimization landscape is too difficult and it needs the MLE term to help capture the location of the optimal decision.

Training SO-EBM with CD-LD achieves similar performance but incurs longer training time. Importance sampling works well due to the low-dimensional optimization problem, resulting in better efficiency. Even with CD-LD, SO-EBM is still much faster than DFL-QPTH.
